content:
Key Findings

  • Burnley Borough Council's spending data for 2021/22 to 2025/26 totals £355.2M.
  • The council engaged with 4,485 unique suppliers during this five-year period.
  • Analysis of the identified "top risk suppliers" shows all of them currently have a risk score of 0.
  • The supplier with the highest individual spend among the listed low-risk providers is RICHMOND & KERSHAW, receiving £509K.
  • STEPPING STONE PROJECT LTD also represents a significant spend, totalling £461K, with a risk score of 0.

Understanding Burnley's Supplier Landscape

Burnley Borough Council manages a substantial financial portfolio, with total spending analysed reaching £355.2M over five financial years. This extensive expenditure covers the period from 2021/22 to 2025/26. The council's operations require engagement with a wide array of external organisations. Our analysis shows Burnley Borough Council has worked with 4,485 unique suppliers during this timeframe. This large number of distinct providers highlights the complexity and breadth of the council's procurement activities.

Understanding who these suppliers are and the nature of their engagement is crucial for transparency. Public spending data allows citizens to scrutinise how their local council allocates funds. AI DOGE's platform aims to make this information accessible, enabling a clearer picture of the financial relationships between the council and its many service providers and contractors.

Absence of High Risk Among Key Suppliers

AI DOGE conducts analysis to identify potential supplier risks within council spending data. For Burnley Borough Council, a review of what were identified as "top risk suppliers" revealed a notable finding. Despite being flagged for deeper scrutiny, all of these specific suppliers were assigned a risk score of 0. This indicates that based on the available data and our current methodology, these particular organisations do not present an elevated risk profile to the council.

The concept of supplier risk encompasses various factors, including financial stability, compliance, and service delivery history. A risk score of 0 suggests a low level of concern in these areas for the identified suppliers. It is important for councils to continuously monitor their supplier base to ensure value for money and mitigate any potential issues that could impact public services. The following table details these suppliers, their associated spend, and their current risk score:

Supplier Name Spend (£) Risk Score
ASPEN VALLEY BOARDING KENNELS & CATTERY £101K 0
CENTRICA BUSINESS SOLUTIONS £168K 0
CLOSOMAT LTD £364K 0
FRED HAMER FUNERAL SERVICES £62K 0
Hyndburn Borough Council £319K 0
RICHMOND & KERSHAW £509K 0
STEPPING STONE PROJECT LTD £461K 0
T H HORN LTD £146K 0
THE ORMEROD HOTEL £47K 0
UK FUELS LTD £63K

Significant Spending with Low-Risk Providers

While the identified suppliers show a risk score of 0, the financial commitments to some of these organisations are substantial. For instance, RICHMOND & KERSHAW received £509K from Burnley Borough Council. This significant expenditure highlights the importance of these suppliers to the council's operations, even with their low-risk classification. Similarly, STEPPING STONE PROJECT LTD accounts for £461K in spending, providing services that are evidently critical to the council's functions.

Other providers like CLOSOMAT LTD with £364K and Hyndburn Borough Council with £319K also represent considerable financial outlays. These figures underscore the need for ongoing transparency and oversight, regardless of current risk scores. Public funds are being directed to these organisations to deliver essential services, from kennelling services by ASPEN VALLEY BOARDING KENNELS & CATTERY (£101K) to fuel supplies from UK FUELS LTD (£63K). Continuous monitoring ensures that these partnerships remain efficient and accountable to the public.

What You Can Do

  • Submit a Freedom of Information request to Burnley Borough Council about their supplier risk assessment processes and criteria.
  • Attend council committee meetings (they are open to the public) to hear discussions on procurement and financial management.
  • Check the council's published contracts register for details on specific agreements with suppliers.
  • Write to your local councillor about the council's spending with specific suppliers and the transparency of risk assessments.
  • Visit aidoge.co.uk to explore the full spending data for Burnley Borough Council and other Lancashire councils yourself.
This article is based on Burnley Borough Council's published spending data covering 2021/22 to 2025/26. All figures are drawn directly from transactions published under the Local Government Transparency Code. Analysis by AI DOGE.
